headline: Chips Nurse Holiday Wounds as Healthcare Leads, Earnings Season Looms
body_html:
Markets reopen Monday morning carrying the residue of a bruising Thursday session for semiconductors, the last full trading day before Friday's Fourth of July holiday closure. The sector shed more than seven percent on a weighted basis, dragging the tape even as more than half the universe closed higher — 218 names advanced against 177 decliners. Healthcare was the clear safe harbour, gaining 2.65 percent, while Consumer and Software each added roughly a percentage point. Now investors face a fresh week with no buffer: whatever moves overnight and in pre-market will set the tone cold.

The chip damage was concentrated but severe. Vicor Corporation plunged 19.2 percent and Axcelis Technologies fell nearly as much, with Alpha and Omega Semiconductor, Sandisk, Teradyne, and Amkor Technology each shedding between 13 and 15 percent. Commentary circulating since Thursday points to a pre-holiday de-risking dynamic in the group ahead of what promises to be a chipmaker-heavy earnings calendar. Samsung's results, expected imminently, are being watched as a first read on semiconductor demand globally.

Geopolitical crosscurrents add complexity to Monday's open. Oil prices are drifting lower after OPEC+ agreed to raise output targets, even as analysts question member capacity and the physical market continues to absorb the supply disruption stemming from the Iran conflict. Shipping data showing Japan-linked vessels departing the Strait of Hormuz is worth monitoring. The NATO summit in Ankara, attended by key allies amid street protests, rounds out a macro backdrop that argues for caution at the bell.

briefs.headline: Samsung Earnings Set the Tone for Chip Bulls and Bears
briefs.body: Samsung's imminent quarterly report is shaping up as the opening act of a closely watched technology earnings season. Investors rattled by Thursday's semiconductor rout will treat the results as a proxy for global chip demand, making the release one of the single most consequential data points for sector positioning this week.

briefs.headline: OPEC+ Output Pledge Pushes Crude Lower, but Delivery Doubts Linger
briefs.body: Oil eased into the long weekend after OPEC+ members agreed to lift production targets, cooling some of the geopolitical premium built into prices. Analysts are skeptical, however, questioning whether key producers can actually meet the higher quotas and who among global buyers will absorb additional barrels in a slowing demand environment.

briefs.headline: Hormuz Shipping Movement Signals Cautious Normalisation
briefs.body: Vessel-tracking data showing more than ten Japan-affiliated ships clearing the Strait of Hormuz suggests some commercial traffic is cautiously returning to the critical waterway. The corridor remains a focal point for energy-market participants given the supply disruptions that followed the Iran conflict earlier this year.

briefs.headline: Surgical and Orthopaedic Device Names Anchor Healthcare Rally
briefs.body: Intuitive Surgical and Globus Medical rose 5.9 percent and 5.5 percent respectively on Thursday, contributing to Healthcare's sector-best gain of 2.65 percent. The moves underscore a rotation into procedure-driven medtech as investors sought defensive ballast amid the semiconductor selloff heading into the holiday.

briefs.headline: Financial-Data Firms Post Strong Gains on Thursday
briefs.body: Equifax and S&P Global each surged roughly six percent, making Financial Data one of the standout sub-sectors of the session. The parallel advance points to renewed appetite for high-quality data and analytics franchises, a theme that often gathers momentum as earnings season draws near and investors seek earnings-visibility anchors.

briefs.headline: NATO Ankara Summit Opens Amid Street Unrest in Turkey
briefs.body: Alliance leaders are convening in Ankara for a summit expected to address burden-sharing and regional security, with more than 100 protesters detained as leftist groups staged demonstrations in the host city. The gathering adds a geopolitical variable to Monday's macro backdrop, particularly given Turkey's strategic position relative to ongoing Middle East tensions.
